quick_facts.label: Typical format
quick_facts.value: MSW is commonly offered as a two-year, four-semester postgraduate programme. For example, SNDT Women’s University describes its on-campus MSW as two years/four semesters, while the Central University of Tamil Nadu describes its MSW as a regular two-year full-time programme.

quick_facts.label: Entry qualification
quick_facts.value: Entry requirements are institution-specific, but official examples admit graduates from any discipline. SNDT requires graduation from a recognised university plus its entrance test; the Central University of Tamil Nadu specifies a bachelor’s degree in any discipline and category-specific minimum marks.

quick_facts.label: Practice orientation
quick_facts.value: MSW curricula commonly combine social-work theory, methods of work with individuals, groups and communities, policy and research with supervised fieldwork or placement. SNDT lists fieldwork practicum in every semester and a summer block placement.

quick_facts.label: Admission routes
quick_facts.value: Admission is not uniform across India: universities may use CUET-PG, a university entrance process, or a combination of tests and merit. The Central University of Tamil Nadu lists CUET score as its MSW selection mode, whereas SNDT lists a written test, group discussion and personal interview.

quick_facts.label: Fee context
quick_facts.value: Fees vary sharply by university and mode. As published on their programme pages, SNDT lists approximate on-campus MSW fees of ₹41,765 for Year I and ₹40,940 for Year II, while IGNOU lists ₹36,000 for its full MSW (ODL) programme, excluding examination fees. Applicants should verify the applicable session’s prospectus before applying.

quick_facts.label: Further-study and examination options
quick_facts.value: An MSW can support applications to PhD programmes, UGC-NET in Social Work, and general-degree competitive examinations such as UPSC Civil Services, subject to each examination or university’s current rules. NTA’s UGC-NET materials identify Social Work as subject code 010.

sections.heading: Eligibility: graduation is the usual starting point, but cut-offs differ
paragraphs: For a conventional MSW, applicants should treat a recognised bachelor’s degree as the baseline rather than assume that a BA is the only accepted parent qualification. SNDT Women’s University accepts graduates in any discipline from a recognised university and requires its entrance test. The Central University of Tamil Nadu similarly accepts a bachelor’s degree of at least three years, or an equivalent qualification, in any discipline.

Minimum marks, reservation treatment, final-year candidature and document rules are set by the admitting institution. For example, the Central University of Tamil Nadu publishes minimum qualifying marks of 55% for general-category applicants, 50% for OBC-NCL applicants and 45% for SC/ST applicants; those figures should not be generalised to other universities.
Distance-learning MSW rules can differ from regular programmes. IGNOU’s MSW (ODL) page lists eligibility as 10+2+3 and describes a 24- to 48-month completion window.
bullets: Check that the degree-awarding university and the specific MSW programme meet the recognition and mode requirements relevant to the applicant’s intended employer or next step.

Use the institution’s current admission bulletin—not a generic course listing—to confirm marks, category certificates, entrance-test requirements and whether final-year applicants may apply provisionally.

sections.heading: Duration and study mode
paragraphs: A regular on-campus MSW is generally structured as two academic years across four semesters. SNDT explicitly describes a two-year, four-semester, full-time on-campus degree and a 64-credit structure. The Central University of Tamil Nadu also describes its MSW as a regular two-year full-time postgraduate programme.

Do not assume every MSW has identical attendance, credit, placement or completion rules. An open-and-distance-learning example is IGNOU’s MSW, which has a minimum duration of 24 months and a maximum duration of 48 months.
bullets: Regular example: two years/four semesters, full-time and on campus.
ODL example: minimum 24 months and maximum 48 months at IGNOU.

sections.heading: What students study: professional methods, welfare systems, research and field learning
paragraphs: There is no single institution-by-institution identical MSW syllabus, but official curricula show a consistent professional-practice core. SNDT’s 64-credit MSW includes history and ideology of social work; work with individuals and families, groups and communities; integrated practice; gender and society; social policy and planning; social-work research; management of developmental and welfare services; child rights; health; law; and counselling.

Field education is a substantive component rather than merely an optional internship in the SNDT example: the curriculum includes fieldwork practicum in each semester and a summer block placement after Semester II. Goa University’s published MSW syllabus likewise includes mandatory fieldwork practicum, rural camp, study tour, block placement and a research project across its two-year, four-semester programme.

Students should compare specialisations and practice settings before applying. Universities may organise electives or placements around areas such as community development, health, counselling, livelihoods, human-resource management or social entrepreneurship; availability depends on the institution and cohort.
bullets: Core knowledge: social-work theory, social policy, welfare/development management, rights and law.
Core methods: case/individual work, group work, community practice, communication and counselling.
Evidence skills: social-work research and, in some programmes, a supervised dissertation or research project.
Applied learning: recurring fieldwork, placement and community exposure.

sections.heading: Admission: confirm the university’s route for the relevant session
paragraphs: Admission mechanisms vary by university. The Central University of Tamil Nadu lists CUET score as the selection mode for its MSW. TISS states that admission to its postgraduate master’s programmes for the 2026 cycle is based on CUET-PG scores except for the programmes specifically excluded on its admissions page. By contrast, SNDT describes an institutional process comprising a written test, group discussion and personal interview, followed by a merit list.

Because entrance-paper mapping, participating universities, application windows, category documentation and counselling rules can change by academic year, CUET-PG should be understood as a possible admission pathway—not an automatic requirement for every Indian MSW. Check both the NTA CUET-PG notices and the target university’s programme-level eligibility and selection page.
bullets: For CUET-PG route: verify the current NTA bulletin and the university’s accepted test-paper code and score-use policy.
For institution-run admission: verify whether selection includes a written test, interview, group discussion, qualifying-degree merit, work experience or any combination.
Keep marksheets, degree/provisional certificate, category/EWS/PwBD documents and other specified certificates ready in the prescribed format.

sections.heading: Costs: use published fees only as dated examples
paragraphs: MSW fees are set by the provider and may differ by public/private status, campus, resident category, scholarship eligibility, hostel use and delivery mode. As displayed on SNDT’s programme page when reviewed, its approximate on-campus fees are ₹41,765 for Year I and ₹40,940 for Year II. These are programme-page figures, not a national benchmark.

For a lower-fee distance-learning comparison, IGNOU lists ₹36,000 for the complete MSW (ODL) programme and states that examination fees are not included. The applicable amounts, payment schedule and any additional charges should be checked in the current admission prospectus before committing.
bullets: Compare tuition with application, examination, fieldwork/travel, hostel, insurance, library and other mandatory charges.
Check scholarship and fee-waiver eligibility directly with the university or the National Scholarship Portal where applicable.
Treat all fee figures as time-sensitive and programme-specific.

sections.heading: Next options after MSW
paragraphs: For research progression, MSW holders can apply to relevant PhD programmes subject to the university’s current rules. TISS’s Social Work PhD admissions page, for example, accepts a two-year/four-semester master’s after a three-year bachelor’s degree, or a one-year/two-semester master’s after a four-year bachelor’s degree, with at least 55% aggregate or equivalent grade; it also states that applicable reserved-category relaxations may apply.

For an academic route, UGC-NET includes Social Work as subject code 010. NTA advises candidates to appear in their postgraduate subject, or a related subject where the postgraduate subject is not included in the NET subject list. UGC’s published teacher-qualification regulations identify a relevant master’s degree with 55% marks plus NET/SLET/SET—subject to listed exemptions—as one route for Assistant Professor eligibility in social-science disciplines.

An MSW may also be useful for public-sector and social-sector roles, but a degree alone does not guarantee eligibility for a named post. For example, an AIIMS recruitment notice for Medical Social Service Officer Grade II required a master’s degree in social work plus relevant welfare/health-agency experience. UPSC Civil Services eligibility is based on a qualifying graduate degree rather than an MSW-specific requirement; the current notification for each cycle governs.

State PSC civil-services and welfare-development posts should be checked state by state: post title, domicile/language conditions, age limits, reservation rules, degree requirements and syllabus are set by the relevant state commission or employing department.
bullets: Research and teaching: PhD in Social Work or allied social-science areas; UGC-NET in Social Work.
Competitive examinations: UPSC Civil Services and relevant State PSC examinations, where the candidate meets the current notification’s general and post-specific requirements.

Practice and programme roles: social-work, welfare, health, development, research, monitoring/evaluation and CSR-related roles; employers may require domain experience, field exposure or specialised skills in addition to MSW.
